sections.heading: RESULTS
sections.summary: This section details the results of ex-post evaluations, stakeholder consultations, and impact assessments related to financial data access. It outlines the European Commission's consultations with stakeholders, including responses from individuals and professionals, and the Expert Group's report on open finance. The impact assessment was reviewed by the Regulatory Scrutiny Board and led to policy options focused on enhancing customer trust, legal clarity, standardization, and data sharing mechanisms within the financial sector. This section presents macroeconomic analysis of potential benefits and costs associated with enhanced data sharing in the EU financial sector. It estimates annual benefits between EUR 4.6 billion and EUR 12.4 billion for the EU economy, including EUR 663 million to EUR 2 billion for the financial data economy. The proposal also projects one-off costs up to EUR 2.2 billion and recurring annual costs between EUR 147 million and EUR 465 million. The analysis highlights how improved data access could support sustainable development, inclusive finance, and climate-related investments while addressing challenges in SME access to financial services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is irrelevant to Data Transfer Impact Assessments as it describes consultation outcomes and policy options for financial data access without specifying cross-border data transfer mechanisms, third-party processing, or data transfer safeguards. This section provides economic context for potential data transfer benefits but does not establish specific transfer mechanisms or safeguards required for DTIAs. It is not directly relevant to the technical or legal requirements of a Data Transfer Impact Assessment.

sections.heading: OTHER ELEMENTS
sections.summary: This section outlines implementation plans and monitoring mechanisms to evaluate regulatory effectiveness, emphasizing the Commission's role in assessing the Regulation's impact and reviewing it per Article 31. It details the proposal's structure across nine Titles, each addressing specific aspects of financial data access, including definitions, data holder obligations, customer rights, security requirements, data sharing schemes, and governance frameworks. The section clarifies that financial data access must comply with GDPR for personal data and involves contractual frameworks for data sharing. This section outlines the need for a harmonized framework to facilitate financial data sharing across borders, addressing fragmentation in the financial sector. It emphasizes the importance of customer control over data sharing to overcome reluctance and enable innovation. The section specifies that payment account data is the primary focus, while broader customer data sharing beyond payments is limited by technical and legal barriers. Financial institutions are only legally required to share payment data, and the regulation aims to expand this to other data types to improve personalized
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides context for financial data sharing schemes and their governance, which are critical for DTIAs involving cross-border financial data transfers. It mandates that data sharing schemes must be notified to authorities, include technical interfaces, and ensure data is only shared within the scheme, impacting how data is transferred internationally. This section highlights the need for a harmonized framework to address barriers in cross-border financial data sharing, emphasizing customer control and the scope of data that can be shared. requirements.text: The Commission will assess the impact of this Regulation and will be tasked with reviewing it (Article 31 of the proposal).
The proposal seeks to establish a framework governing access to and use of customer data in finance (financial data access ‘FIDA').

Financial data access refers to the access to and processing of business-to-business and business-to-customer (including consumer) data upon customer request across a wide range of financial services.

Title I sets the subject matter, scope and definitions. Article 1 sets out that the Regulation establishes the rules in line with which certain categories of customer data in finance may be accessed, shared, and used.
Article 2 sets the scope of the Regulation to certain exhaustively described sets of data and lists the firms to which this Regulation applies.
Article 3 sets the terms and definitions that are used for the purposes of this Regulation, including 'data holder', 'data user', 'financial information service provider' and others.

Title II introduces a legal obligation on data holders and governs the way this obligation should be exercised. Article 4 indicates that the data holder must make available to customers the data within the scope of this Regulation based on a request.
Article 5 provides the customer with the right to request that the data holder shares this data with a data user.
Where personal data is concerned, the request must comply with a valid legal basis as referred to in the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) that allows for the processing of personal data.

Article 6 imposes certain obligations on data users receiving data at the request of customers. There should only be access to the customer data made available under Article 5 and this data should be used only for the purposes and the conditions agreed with the customer.
The customer's personalised security credentials should not be accessible to other parties and the data should not be stored for longer than what is necessary.
The sharing of customer data in the scope of this Regulation should be based on the permission of the customer.
The legal obligation on data holders to share customer data should be triggered once the customer has requested their data to be shared with a data user.
Where the processing of personal data is involved, a data user should have a valid lawful basis for processing under Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
The processing of personal data must respect the principles of personal data protection, including lawfulness, fairness and transparency, purpose limitation and data minimisation.
A customer has the right to withdraw the permission given to a data user.
When data processing is necessary for the performance of a contract, a customer should be able to withdraw permissions according to the contractual obligations to which the data subject is party.
When personal data processing is based on consent, a data subject has the right to withdraw his or her consent at any time, as provided for in Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
Financial institutions must provide access to defined categories of data at the request of the customer when acting as data holders.
Data sharing should be based on customer permission when financial institutions act as data users.
Permission dashboards must empower customers to manage permissions in an informed and impartial manner without undue influence.
Data holders must inform data users in real-time of any withdrawal of a permission.
Data users must inform data holders in real-time of new or re-established permissions granted by customers.
Permission dashboards should include a record of withdrawn or expired permissions for up to two years.

Data holders and data users should be part of financial data sharing schemes that develop data and interface standards, joint standardised contractual frameworks, and governance rules for data sharing.
Financial data sharing schemes must comply with Union rules in the area of consumer protection and data protection, privacy, and competition.
Data holders should be able to request reasonable compensation from data users for application programming interfaces.
Financial information service providers must be either legally incorporated in the Union or appoint a legal representative in the Union.
Financial information service providers should be authorised in the jurisdiction of the Member State where their main establishment is located.
Competent authorities should have powers to require data traffic records and seize relevant documents for investigations.

Member States should be required to provide for effective, proportionate and dissuasive administrative sanctions, including periodic penalty payments, and administrative measures for the infringement of provisions of this Regulation.
Member States should lay down specific rules and effective mechanisms regarding the application of periodic penalty payments.

Competent authorities should be empowered to impose periodic penalty payments on financial information service providers and on those members of their management body who are identified as responsible for an ongoing infringement or who are required to comply with an order from an investigating competent authority.
Periodic penalty payments should not prevent competent authorities from imposing subsequent administrative sanctions for the same infringement.
Unless otherwise provided for by Member States, periodic penalty payments should be calculated on a daily basis.
Member States should not be obliged to introduce such enforcement methods in their legal framework nor should competent authorities be compelled to use them if they do not deem it appropriate.

Where Member States choose to empower their competent authorities to use such enforcement methods, they should notify the Commission of such decision and of the relevant measures regulating such powers.

A financial information service provider wishing to have access to data held by a data holder in another Member State, should notify its intention to its competent authority, providing information on the type of data it wishes to access, the financial data sharing scheme of which it is a member and the Member States in which it intends to access the data.
The right to access and transfer all necessary personal data to a new pan-European Personal Pension Product provider under Regulation (EU) 2019/1238
The regulation does not affect the application of EU or national rules of competition of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union
This Regulation is also without prejudice to accessing, sharing and using data without making use of the data access obligations established by this Regulation on a purely contractual basis

sections.summary: When sharing customer data with a data user, the data holder must provide data in a standardized format at the same quality level as the data holder possesses, ensure secure communication with the data user, verify that data users have customer permission, provide customers with a permission dashboard to manage access, and respect trade secrets and intellectual property rights. This section outlines specific technical and procedural requirements for data sharing.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates standardized data formats, secure communication, permission verification, and customer monitoring tools for data sharing, which are critical for ensuring secure cross-border data transfers and compliance with financial data sharing schemes.

sections.source_quote: When making data available pursuant to paragraph 1, the data holder shall: (a) make customer data available to the data user in a format based on generally recognised standards and at least in the same quality available to the data holder; (b) communicate securely with the data user by ensuring an appropriate level of security for the processing and transmission of customer data; (c) request data users to demonstrate that they have obtained the permission of the customer to access the customer data held by the data holder; (d) provide the customer with a permission dashboard to monitor and manage permissions in accordance with Article 8. (e) respect the confidentiality of trade secrets and intellectual property rights when customer data is accessed in accordance with Article 5(1).

requirements.text: The Commission will assess the impact of this Regulation and will be tasked with reviewing it (Article 31 of the proposal).
The proposal seeks to establish a framework governing access to and use of customer data in finance (financial data access ‘FIDA').

Financial data access refers to the access to and processing of business-to-business and business-to-customer (including consumer) data upon customer request across a wide range of financial services.

Title I sets the subject matter, scope and definitions. Article 1 sets out that the Regulation establishes the rules in line with which certain categories of customer data in finance may be accessed, shared, and used.
Article 2 sets the scope of the Regulation to certain exhaustively described sets of data and lists the firms to which this Regulation applies.
Article 3 sets the terms and definitions that are used for the purposes of this Regulation, including 'data holder', 'data user', 'financial information service provider' and others.

Title II introduces a legal obligation on data holders and governs the way this obligation should be exercised. Article 4 indicates that the data holder must make available to customers the data within the scope of this Regulation based on a request.
Article 5 provides the customer with the right to request that the data holder shares this data with a data user.
Where personal data is concerned, the request must comply with a valid legal basis as referred to in the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) that allows for the processing of personal data.

Article 6 imposes certain obligations on data users receiving data at the request of customers. There should only be access to the customer data made available under Article 5 and this data should be used only for the purposes and the conditions agreed with the customer.
The customer's personalised security credentials should not be accessible to other parties and the data should not be stored for longer than what is necessary.
The sharing of customer data in the scope of this Regulation should be based on the permission of the customer.
The legal obligation on data holders to share customer data should be triggered once the customer has requested their data to be shared with a data user.
Where the processing of personal data is involved, a data user should have a valid lawful basis for processing under Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
The processing of personal data must respect the principles of personal data protection, including lawfulness, fairness and transparency, purpose limitation and data minimisation.
A customer has the right to withdraw the permission given to a data user.
When data processing is necessary for the performance of a contract, a customer should be able to withdraw permissions according to the contractual obligations to which the data subject is party.
When personal data processing is based on consent, a data subject has the right to withdraw his or her consent at any time, as provided for in Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
Financial institutions must provide access to defined categories of data at the request of the customer when acting as data holders.
Data sharing should be based on customer permission when financial institutions act as data users.
Permission dashboards must empower customers to manage permissions in an informed and impartial manner without undue influence.
Data holders must inform data users in real-time of any withdrawal of a permission.
Data users must inform data holders in real-time of new or re-established permissions granted by customers.
Permission dashboards should include a record of withdrawn or expired permissions for up to two years.

Data holders and data users should be part of financial data sharing schemes that develop data and interface standards, joint standardised contractual frameworks, and governance rules for data sharing.
Financial data sharing schemes must comply with Union rules in the area of consumer protection and data protection, privacy, and competition.
Data holders should be able to request reasonable compensation from data users for application programming interfaces.
Financial information service providers must be either legally incorporated in the Union or appoint a legal representative in the Union.
Financial information service providers should be authorised in the jurisdiction of the Member State where their main establishment is located.
Competent authorities should have powers to require data traffic records and seize relevant documents for investigations.

Member States should be required to provide for effective, proportionate and dissuasive administrative sanctions, including periodic penalty payments, and administrative measures for the infringement of provisions of this Regulation.
Member States should lay down specific rules and effective mechanisms regarding the application of periodic penalty payments.

Competent authorities should be empowered to impose periodic penalty payments on financial information service providers and on those members of their management body who are identified as responsible for an ongoing infringement or who are required to comply with an order from an investigating competent authority.
Periodic penalty payments should not prevent competent authorities from imposing subsequent administrative sanctions for the same infringement.
Unless otherwise provided for by Member States, periodic penalty payments should be calculated on a daily basis.
Member States should not be obliged to introduce such enforcement methods in their legal framework nor should competent authorities be compelled to use them if they do not deem it appropriate.

Where Member States choose to empower their competent authorities to use such enforcement methods, they should notify the Commission of such decision and of the relevant measures regulating such powers.

A financial information service provider wishing to have access to data held by a data holder in another Member State, should notify its intention to its competent authority, providing information on the type of data it wishes to access, the financial data sharing scheme of which it is a member and the Member States in which it intends to access the data.
The right to access and transfer all necessary personal data to a new pan-European Personal Pension Product provider under Regulation (EU) 2019/1238
The regulation does not affect the application of EU or national rules of competition of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union
This Regulation is also without prejudice to accessing, sharing and using data without making use of the data access obligations established by this Regulation on a purely contractual basis

sections.summary: This section details the results of ex-post evaluations, stakeholder consultations, and impact assessments related to financial data access. It outlines the European Commission's consultations with stakeholders, including responses from individuals and professionals, and the Expert Group's report on open finance. The impact assessment was reviewed by the Regulatory Scrutiny Board and led to policy options focused on enhancing customer trust, legal clarity, standardization, and data sharing mechanisms within the financial sector. This section presents macroeconomic analysis of potential benefits and costs associated with enhanced data sharing in the EU financial sector. It estimates annual benefits between EUR 4.6 billion and EUR 12.4 billion for the EU economy, including EUR 663 million to EUR 2 billion for the financial data economy. The proposal also projects one-off costs up to EUR 2.2 billion and recurring annual costs between EUR 147 million and EUR 465 million. The analysis highlights how improved data access could support sustainable development, inclusive finance, and climate-related investments while addressing challenges in SME access to financial services.

requirements.text: The Commission will assess the impact of this Regulation and will be tasked with reviewing it (Article 31 of the proposal).
The proposal seeks to establish a framework governing access to and use of customer data in finance (financial data access ‘FIDA').

Financial data access refers to the access to and processing of business-to-business and business-to-customer (including consumer) data upon customer request across a wide range of financial services.

Title I sets the subject matter, scope and definitions. Article 1 sets out that the Regulation establishes the rules in line with which certain categories of customer data in finance may be accessed, shared, and used.
Article 2 sets the scope of the Regulation to certain exhaustively described sets of data and lists the firms to which this Regulation applies.
Article 3 sets the terms and definitions that are used for the purposes of this Regulation, including 'data holder', 'data user', 'financial information service provider' and others.

Title II introduces a legal obligation on data holders and governs the way this obligation should be exercised. Article 4 indicates that the data holder must make available to customers the data within the scope of this Regulation based on a request.
Article 5 provides the customer with the right to request that the data holder shares this data with a data user.
Where personal data is concerned, the request must comply with a valid legal basis as referred to in the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) that allows for the processing of personal data.

Article 6 imposes certain obligations on data users receiving data at the request of customers. There should only be access to the customer data made available under Article 5 and this data should be used only for the purposes and the conditions agreed with the customer.
The customer's personalised security credentials should not be accessible to other parties and the data should not be stored for longer than what is necessary.
The sharing of customer data in the scope of this Regulation should be based on the permission of the customer.
The legal obligation on data holders to share customer data should be triggered once the customer has requested their data to be shared with a data user.
Where the processing of personal data is involved, a data user should have a valid lawful basis for processing under Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
The processing of personal data must respect the principles of personal data protection, including lawfulness, fairness and transparency, purpose limitation and data minimisation.
A customer has the right to withdraw the permission given to a data user.
When data processing is necessary for the performance of a contract, a customer should be able to withdraw permissions according to the contractual obligations to which the data subject is party.
When personal data processing is based on consent, a data subject has the right to withdraw his or her consent at any time, as provided for in Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
Financial institutions must provide access to defined categories of data at the request of the customer when acting as data holders.
Data sharing should be based on customer permission when financial institutions act as data users.
Permission dashboards must empower customers to manage permissions in an informed and impartial manner without undue influence.
Data holders must inform data users in real-time of any withdrawal of a permission.
Data users must inform data holders in real-time of new or re-established permissions granted by customers.
Permission dashboards should include a record of withdrawn or expired permissions for up to two years.

Data holders and data users should be part of financial data sharing schemes that develop data and interface standards, joint standardised contractual frameworks, and governance rules for data sharing.
Financial data sharing schemes must comply with Union rules in the area of consumer protection and data protection, privacy, and competition.
Data holders should be able to request reasonable compensation from data users for application programming interfaces.
Financial information service providers must be either legally incorporated in the Union or appoint a legal representative in the Union.
Financial information service providers should be authorised in the jurisdiction of the Member State where their main establishment is located.
Competent authorities should have powers to require data traffic records and seize relevant documents for investigations.

Member States should be required to provide for effective, proportionate and dissuasive administrative sanctions, including periodic penalty payments, and administrative measures for the infringement of provisions of this Regulation.
Member States should lay down specific rules and effective mechanisms regarding the application of periodic penalty payments.

Competent authorities should be empowered to impose periodic penalty payments on financial information service providers and on those members of their management body who are identified as responsible for an ongoing infringement or who are required to comply with an order from an investigating competent authority.
Periodic penalty payments should not prevent competent authorities from imposing subsequent administrative sanctions for the same infringement.
Unless otherwise provided for by Member States, periodic penalty payments should be calculated on a daily basis.
Member States should not be obliged to introduce such enforcement methods in their legal framework nor should competent authorities be compelled to use them if they do not deem it appropriate.

Where Member States choose to empower their competent authorities to use such enforcement methods, they should notify the Commission of such decision and of the relevant measures regulating such powers.

A financial information service provider wishing to have access to data held by a data holder in another Member State, should notify its intention to its competent authority, providing information on the type of data it wishes to access, the financial data sharing scheme of which it is a member and the Member States in which it intends to access the data.
The right to access and transfer all necessary personal data to a new pan-European Personal Pension Product provider under Regulation (EU) 2019/1238
The regulation does not affect the application of EU or national rules of competition of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union
This Regulation is also without prejudice to accessing, sharing and using data without making use of the data access obligations established by this Regulation on a purely contractual basis

sections.heading: The competent authority may withdraw an authorisation issued to a financial
sections.summary: The competent authority may revoke authorization for a financial information service provider if the provider has not used the authorization within 12 months, has renounced it, ceased business for over six months, obtained authorization through false statements, no longer meets authorization conditions, fails to report major developments, or poses a consumer protection or data security risk. The authority must provide reasons for withdrawal and notify affected parties, publishing the withdrawal in anonymized form.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which authorization can be withdrawn, impacting data transfer assessments by requiring specific justifications and public disclosure for authorization revocations related to cross-border financial data processing.

requirements.text: The competent authority may withdraw an authorisation issued to a financial information service provider only if the provider: (a) does not make use of the authorisation within 12 months, expressly renounces the authorisation or has ceased to engage in business for more than 6 months; (b) has obtained the authorisation through false statements or any other irregular means; (c) no longer meets the conditions for granting the authorisation or fails to inform the competent authority on major developments in this respect; (d) would constitute a risk to consumer protection and the security of data.
The competent authority shall give reasons for any withdrawal of an authorisation and shall inform those concerned accordingly.
The competent authority shall make public the withdrawal of an authorisation, in an anonymised version.

sections.summary: This section outlines implementation plans and monitoring mechanisms to evaluate regulatory effectiveness, emphasizing the Commission's role in assessing the Regulation's impact and reviewing it per Article 31. It details the proposal's structure across nine Titles, each addressing specific aspects of financial data access, including definitions, data holder obligations, customer rights, security requirements, data sharing schemes, and governance frameworks. The section clarifies that financial data access must comply with GDPR for personal data and involves contractual frameworks for data sharing. This section outlines the need for a harmonized framework to facilitate financial data sharing across borders, addressing fragmentation in the financial sector. It emphasizes the importance of customer control over data sharing to overcome reluctance and enable innovation. The section specifies that payment account data is the primary focus, while broader customer data sharing beyond payments is limited by technical and legal barriers. Financial institutions are only legally required to share payment data, and the regulation aims to expand this to other data types to improve personalized

requirements.text: The Commission will assess the impact of this Regulation and will be tasked with reviewing it (Article 31 of the proposal).
The proposal seeks to establish a framework governing access to and use of customer data in finance (financial data access ‘FIDA').

Financial data access refers to the access to and processing of business-to-business and business-to-customer (including consumer) data upon customer request across a wide range of financial services.

Title I sets the subject matter, scope and definitions. Article 1 sets out that the Regulation establishes the rules in line with which certain categories of customer data in finance may be accessed, shared, and used.
Article 2 sets the scope of the Regulation to certain exhaustively described sets of data and lists the firms to which this Regulation applies.
Article 3 sets the terms and definitions that are used for the purposes of this Regulation, including 'data holder', 'data user', 'financial information service provider' and others.

Title II introduces a legal obligation on data holders and governs the way this obligation should be exercised. Article 4 indicates that the data holder must make available to customers the data within the scope of this Regulation based on a request.
Article 5 provides the customer with the right to request that the data holder shares this data with a data user.
Where personal data is concerned, the request must comply with a valid legal basis as referred to in the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) that allows for the processing of personal data.

Article 6 imposes certain obligations on data users receiving data at the request of customers. There should only be access to the customer data made available under Article 5 and this data should be used only for the purposes and the conditions agreed with the customer.
The customer's personalised security credentials should not be accessible to other parties and the data should not be stored for longer than what is necessary.
The sharing of customer data in the scope of this Regulation should be based on the permission of the customer.
The legal obligation on data holders to share customer data should be triggered once the customer has requested their data to be shared with a data user.
Where the processing of personal data is involved, a data user should have a valid lawful basis for processing under Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
The processing of personal data must respect the principles of personal data protection, including lawfulness, fairness and transparency, purpose limitation and data minimisation.
A customer has the right to withdraw the permission given to a data user.
When data processing is necessary for the performance of a contract, a customer should be able to withdraw permissions according to the contractual obligations to which the data subject is party.
When personal data processing is based on consent, a data subject has the right to withdraw his or her consent at any time, as provided for in Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
Financial institutions must provide access to defined categories of data at the request of the customer when acting as data holders.
Data sharing should be based on customer permission when financial institutions act as data users.
Permission dashboards must empower customers to manage permissions in an informed and impartial manner without undue influence.
Data holders must inform data users in real-time of any withdrawal of a permission.
Data users must inform data holders in real-time of new or re-established permissions granted by customers.
Permission dashboards should include a record of withdrawn or expired permissions for up to two years.

Data holders and data users should be part of financial data sharing schemes that develop data and interface standards, joint standardised contractual frameworks, and governance rules for data sharing.
Financial data sharing schemes must comply with Union rules in the area of consumer protection and data protection, privacy, and competition.
Data holders should be able to request reasonable compensation from data users for application programming interfaces.
Financial information service providers must be either legally incorporated in the Union or appoint a legal representative in the Union.
Financial information service providers should be authorised in the jurisdiction of the Member State where their main establishment is located.
Competent authorities should have powers to require data traffic records and seize relevant documents for investigations.

Member States should be required to provide for effective, proportionate and dissuasive administrative sanctions, including periodic penalty payments, and administrative measures for the infringement of provisions of this Regulation.
Member States should lay down specific rules and effective mechanisms regarding the application of periodic penalty payments.

Competent authorities should be empowered to impose periodic penalty payments on financial information service providers and on those members of their management body who are identified as responsible for an ongoing infringement or who are required to comply with an order from an investigating competent authority.
Periodic penalty payments should not prevent competent authorities from imposing subsequent administrative sanctions for the same infringement.
Unless otherwise provided for by Member States, periodic penalty payments should be calculated on a daily basis.
Member States should not be obliged to introduce such enforcement methods in their legal framework nor should competent authorities be compelled to use them if they do not deem it appropriate.

Where Member States choose to empower their competent authorities to use such enforcement methods, they should notify the Commission of such decision and of the relevant measures regulating such powers.

A financial information service provider wishing to have access to data held by a data holder in another Member State, should notify its intention to its competent authority, providing information on the type of data it wishes to access, the financial data sharing scheme of which it is a member and the Member States in which it intends to access the data.
The right to access and transfer all necessary personal data to a new pan-European Personal Pension Product provider under Regulation (EU) 2019/1238
The regulation does not affect the application of EU or national rules of competition of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union
This Regulation is also without prejudice to accessing, sharing and using data without making use of the data access obligations established by this Regulation on a purely contractual basis

sections.heading: Competent authorities exchanging information with other competent authorities
sections.summary: This section details the results of ex-post evaluations, stakeholder consultations, and impact assessments related to financial data access. It outlines the European Commission's consultations with stakeholders, including responses from individuals and professionals, and the Expert Group's report on open finance. The impact assessment was reviewed by the Regulatory Scrutiny Board and led to policy options focused on enhancing customer trust, legal clarity, standardization, and data sharing mechanisms within the financial sector. This section presents macroeconomic analysis of potential benefits and costs associated with enhanced data sharing in the EU financial sector. It estimates annual benefits between EUR 4.6 billion and EUR 12.4 billion for the EU economy, including EUR 663 million to EUR 2 billion for the financial data economy. The proposal also projects one-off costs up to EUR 2.2 billion and recurring annual costs between EUR 147 million and EUR 465 million. The analysis highlights how improved data access could support sustainable development, inclusive finance, and climate-related investments while addressing challenges in SME access to financial services.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is irrelevant to Data Transfer Impact Assessments as it describes consultation outcomes and policy options for financial data access without specifying cross-border data transfer mechanisms, third-party processing, or data transfer safeguards. This section provides economic context for potential data transfer benefits but does not establish specific transfer mechanisms or safeguards required for DTIAs. It is not directly relevant to the technical or legal requirements of a Data Transfer Impact Assessment.

requirements.text: The European Commission launched a call for evidence on financial data access, gathering 79 responses by 2 August 2022.
The Commission conducted a public consultation on the review of the revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2) and financial data access, receiving responses by 2 August 2022.
The Commission held a targeted consultation on financial data access and data sharing in the financial sector, gathering 94 responses from professional stakeholders by 5 July 2022.
The Commission received a report on open finance from the Expert Group on the European Financial Data Space on 24 October 2022.
The impact assessment was submitted to the Regulatory Scrutiny Board (RSB) on 3 February 2023 and approved on 3 March 2023.
The total annual benefits for the EU economy produced by enhanced access to and sharing of data in the EU financial sector ranges between EUR 4.6 billion and EUR 12.4 billion
The overall estimated cost of the proposal could be up to a range of EUR 2.2 billion to EUR 2.4 billion in one-off costs and between EUR 147 million to EUR 465 million in recurring annual costs

sections.summary: This section outlines implementation plans and monitoring mechanisms to evaluate regulatory effectiveness, emphasizing the Commission's role in assessing the Regulation's impact and reviewing it per Article 31. It details the proposal's structure across nine Titles, each addressing specific aspects of financial data access, including definitions, data holder obligations, customer rights, security requirements, data sharing schemes, and governance frameworks. The section clarifies that financial data access must comply with GDPR for personal data and involves contractual frameworks for data sharing. This section outlines the need for a harmonized framework to facilitate financial data sharing across borders, addressing fragmentation in the financial sector. It emphasizes the importance of customer control over data sharing to overcome reluctance and enable innovation. The section specifies that payment account data is the primary focus, while broader customer data sharing beyond payments is limited by technical and legal barriers. Financial institutions are only legally required to share payment data, and the regulation aims to expand this to other data types to improve personalized
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides context for financial data sharing schemes and their governance, which are critical for DTIAs involving cross-border financial data transfers. It mandates that data sharing schemes must be notified to authorities, include technical interfaces, and ensure data is only shared within the scheme, impacting how data is transferred internationally. This section highlights the need for a harmonized framework to address barriers in cross-border financial data sharing, emphasizing customer control and the scope of data that can be shared. requirements.text: The Commission will assess the impact of this Regulation and will be tasked with reviewing it (Article 31 of the proposal).
The proposal seeks to establish a framework governing access to and use of customer data in finance (financial data access ‘FIDA').

Financial data access refers to the access to and processing of business-to-business and business-to-customer (including consumer) data upon customer request across a wide range of financial services.

Title I sets the subject matter, scope and definitions. Article 1 sets out that the Regulation establishes the rules in line with which certain categories of customer data in finance may be accessed, shared, and used.
Article 2 sets the scope of the Regulation to certain exhaustively described sets of data and lists the firms to which this Regulation applies.
Article 3 sets the terms and definitions that are used for the purposes of this Regulation, including 'data holder', 'data user', 'financial information service provider' and others.

Title II introduces a legal obligation on data holders and governs the way this obligation should be exercised. Article 4 indicates that the data holder must make available to customers the data within the scope of this Regulation based on a request.
Article 5 provides the customer with the right to request that the data holder shares this data with a data user.
Where personal data is concerned, the request must comply with a valid legal basis as referred to in the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) that allows for the processing of personal data.

Article 6 imposes certain obligations on data users receiving data at the request of customers. There should only be access to the customer data made available under Article 5 and this data should be used only for the purposes and the conditions agreed with the customer.
The customer's personalised security credentials should not be accessible to other parties and the data should not be stored for longer than what is necessary.
The sharing of customer data in the scope of this Regulation should be based on the permission of the customer.
The legal obligation on data holders to share customer data should be triggered once the customer has requested their data to be shared with a data user.
Where the processing of personal data is involved, a data user should have a valid lawful basis for processing under Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
The processing of personal data must respect the principles of personal data protection, including lawfulness, fairness and transparency, purpose limitation and data minimisation.
A customer has the right to withdraw the permission given to a data user.
When data processing is necessary for the performance of a contract, a customer should be able to withdraw permissions according to the contractual obligations to which the data subject is party.
When personal data processing is based on consent, a data subject has the right to withdraw his or her consent at any time, as provided for in Regulation (EU) 2016/679.
Financial institutions must provide access to defined categories of data at the request of the customer when acting as data holders.
Data sharing should be based on customer permission when financial institutions act as data users.
Permission dashboards must empower customers to manage permissions in an informed and impartial manner without undue influence.
Data holders must inform data users in real-time of any withdrawal of a permission.
Data users must inform data holders in real-time of new or re-established permissions granted by customers.
Permission dashboards should include a record of withdrawn or expired permissions for up to two years.

Data holders and data users should be part of financial data sharing schemes that develop data and interface standards, joint standardised contractual frameworks, and governance rules for data sharing.
Financial data sharing schemes must comply with Union rules in the area of consumer protection and data protection, privacy, and competition.
Data holders should be able to request reasonable compensation from data users for application programming interfaces.
Financial information service providers must be either legally incorporated in the Union or appoint a legal representative in the Union.
Financial information service providers should be authorised in the jurisdiction of the Member State where their main establishment is located.
Competent authorities should have powers to require data traffic records and seize relevant documents for investigations.

Member States should be required to provide for effective, proportionate and dissuasive administrative sanctions, including periodic penalty payments, and administrative measures for the infringement of provisions of this Regulation.
Member States should lay down specific rules and effective mechanisms regarding the application of periodic penalty payments.

Competent authorities should be empowered to impose periodic penalty payments on financial information service providers and on those members of their management body who are identified as responsible for an ongoing infringement or who are required to comply with an order from an investigating competent authority.
Periodic penalty payments should not prevent competent authorities from imposing subsequent administrative sanctions for the same infringement.
Unless otherwise provided for by Member States, periodic penalty payments should be calculated on a daily basis.
Member States should not be obliged to introduce such enforcement methods in their legal framework nor should competent authorities be compelled to use them if they do not deem it appropriate.

Where Member States choose to empower their competent authorities to use such enforcement methods, they should notify the Commission of such decision and of the relevant measures regulating such powers.

A financial information service provider wishing to have access to data held by a data holder in another Member State, should notify its intention to its competent authority, providing information on the type of data it wishes to access, the financial data sharing scheme of which it is a member and the Member States in which it intends to access the data.
The right to access and transfer all necessary personal data to a new pan-European Personal Pension Product provider under Regulation (EU) 2019/1238
The regulation does not affect the application of EU or national rules of competition of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union
This Regulation is also without prejudice to accessing, sharing and using data without making use of the data access obligations established by this Regulation on a purely contractual basis

sections.heading: A delegated act adopted pursuant to Article 11, shall enter into force only if no
sections.summary: A delegated act adopted under Article 11 enters into force only if no objections are raised by the European Parliament or Council within three months of notification, or if both institutions inform the Commission they will not object. The period may be extended by three months on either institution's initiative.

requirements.text: A delegated act adopted pursuant to Article 11 shall enter into force only if no objection has been expressed either by the European Parliament or by the Council within a period of three months of notification of that act to the European Parliament and to the Council
or if, before the expiry of that period, the European Parliament and the Council have both informed the Commission that they will not object
That period shall be extended by three months on the initiative of the European Parliament or of the Council
